era_context: The year 1971 falls within the mature Cold War period, characterised by superpower détente, the winding down of American ground involvement in Vietnam, and the final collapse of the post-war Bretton Woods monetary order. Decolonisation's second wave was reshaping South Asia and the Persian Gulf, while postcolonial Africa experienced coups and authoritarian consolidation. China remained convulsed by the late Cultural Revolution but was beginning its pivot toward engagement with the West. The global economy stood at a hinge point: the Nixon Shock ended dollar-gold convertibility, inaugurating the era of floating exchange rates. Technologically, the microprocessor revolution was about to begin, and the space programmes of both superpowers continued to produce landmark achievements.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Internal developments in Central Asian and Caucasian Soviet republics beyond all-Union reporting
Pacific Island nations and Melanesian societies receive negligible coverage
Indigenous experiences in the Americas, Australia, and the Arctic are underrepresented relative to their significance
Internal politics of smaller West and Central African states are thinly documented in available English-language sources
Southeast Asian nations beyond the Vietnam War theatre (e.g., Burma, Laos internal dynamics) receive disproportionately less attention

events.title: Idi Amin seizes power in Uganda
events.description: On 25 January 1971, Major General Idi Amin Dada overthrew President Milton Obote in a military coup while Obote was attending a Commonwealth summit in Singapore. Amin, commander of the Ugandan army, seized control of Kampala with support from disaffected military units, particularly those from the West Nile region. He initially enjoyed British and Israeli support, as Obote had been moving toward closer ties with the Soviet Union and had nationalised foreign businesses. Amin's rule would rapidly deteriorate into one of the most brutal dictatorships in African history, with an estimated 100,000–500,000 killed during his eight-year regime. The coup reflected the fragility of post-independence African states where ethnic military factions could overpower civilian institutions.
key_figures: Idi Amin Dada (Major General, coup leader)
Milton Obote (deposed President of Uganda)

sources.name: Contemporary reporting in The Times (London) and New York Times, January–February 1971
Kyemba, Henry, A State of Blood: The Inside Story of Idi Amin (1977)
Mutibwa, Phares, Uganda Since Independence: A Story of Unfulfilled Hopes (1992)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented coup with immediate international press coverage and diplomatic records.

events.title: Bangladesh Liberation War and Indo-Pakistani War
events.description: Following the Pakistani military's refusal to honour the Awami League's landslide victory in the December 1970 elections, and the brutal crackdown of Operation Searchlight launched on 25 March 1971, East Pakistan erupted into a full-scale liberation war. Sheikh Mujibur Rahman declared independence on 26 March before being arrested. The Pakistani military campaign targeted Bengali intellectuals, Hindu minorities, and suspected nationalists, producing what scholars estimate as between 300,000 and 3 million deaths and driving approximately 10 million refugees into India. The Mukti Bahini guerrilla forces, with increasing Indian support, fought throughout the year. On 3 December, Pakistan launched pre-emptive air strikes on Indian airfields, triggering a formal Indo-Pakistani war. India's decisive military intervention on both western and eastern fronts led to the surrender of 93,000 Pakistani troops at Dhaka on 16 December 1971, and the birth of Bangladesh as an independent state.
key_figures: Sheikh Mujibur Rahman (leader of the Awami League, founding father of Bangladesh)
Yahya Khan (President of Pakistan)
Indira Gandhi (Prime Minister of India)
Lt. Gen. A.A.K. Niazi (Pakistani commander, Eastern Command)
Lt. Gen. Jagjit Singh Aurora (Indian commander, Eastern Front)
Zulfikar Ali Bhutto (leader of Pakistan People's Party)
Gen. M.A.G. Osmani (Commander, Mukti Bahini forces)

sources.name: Instrument of Surrender signed at Dhaka, 16 December 1971
Hamoodur Rahman Commission Report (1974, declassified 2000)
Sisson, Richard and Rose, Leo E., War and Secession: Pakistan, India, and the Creation of Bangladesh (1990)
Bass, Gary J., The Blood Telegram: Nixon, Kissinger, and a Forgotten Genocide (2013)
U.S. State Department cables (Archer Blood 'Blood Telegram', April 1971)

events.certainty_note: Massively documented conflict with military records, diplomatic cables, extensive journalism, and post-war commissions. Death toll figures remain contested.

events.title: Pentagon Papers published
events.description: On 13 June 1971, the New York Times began publishing excerpts from the 'History of U.S. Decision-Making on Vietnam Policy, 1945–1967,' a 7,000-page classified study commissioned by Secretary of Defense Robert McNamara. The documents were leaked by Daniel Ellsberg, a former RAND Corporation analyst and Defence Department official who had grown disillusioned with the war. The papers revealed systematic government deception about the scope and prospects of American involvement in Vietnam across four administrations. The Nixon administration obtained a temporary restraining order, but on 30 June the Supreme Court ruled 6–3 in New York Times Co. v. United States that prior restraint was unconstitutional. The affair deepened public distrust of government, contributed to Nixon's creation of the 'Plumbers' unit (which would lead to Watergate), and became a landmark case for press freedom.
key_figures: Daniel Ellsberg (former RAND analyst, whistleblower)
Neil Sheehan (New York Times reporter who received the documents)
Robert McNamara (former Secretary of Defense who commissioned the study)
Richard Nixon (President of the United States)

sources.name: New York Times Co. v. United States, 403 U.S. 713 (1971)
The Pentagon Papers: The Defense Department History of United States Decisionmaking on Vietnam (Senator Gravel Edition, 1971)
Ellsberg, Daniel, Secrets: A Memoir of Vietnam and the Pentagon Papers (2002)

events.certainty_note: Published documents, Supreme Court ruling, and extensive contemporaneous press coverage.

events.title: Nixon Shock: end of dollar-gold convertibility
events.description: On 15 August 1971, President Nixon announced a package of economic measures that effectively ended the Bretton Woods system of fixed exchange rates anchored to gold. The key element was the suspension of dollar convertibility into gold at the fixed rate of $35 per ounce, a pillar of the international monetary system since 1944. Nixon also imposed a temporary 90-day wage-price freeze and a 10% import surcharge. The decision was driven by accelerating U.S. inflation, a deteriorating balance of payments, and the draining of American gold reserves as foreign central banks (particularly France) redeemed dollars for gold. The Smithsonian Agreement of December 1971 attempted to restore fixed rates with a devalued dollar, but this proved temporary; by 1973 all major currencies were floating. The Nixon Shock fundamentally restructured the global financial system and inaugurated an era of currency volatility, financialisation, and the dollar's role as a fiat reserve currency.
key_figures: Richard Nixon (President of the United States)
John Connally (Secretary of the Treasury)
Paul Volcker (Under Secretary of the Treasury for International Monetary Affairs)
Arthur Burns (Chairman, Federal Reserve)

sources.name: Nixon's televised address, 15 August 1971 (text in Public Papers of the Presidents)
Smithsonian Agreement, December 1971 (IMF Archives)
Eichengreen, Barry, Globalizing Capital: A History of the International Monetary System (2008, 2nd ed.)
Gowa, Joanne, Closing the Gold Window: Domestic Politics and the End of Bretton Woods (1983)

events.certainty_note: Televised presidential announcement with complete documentary trail in government and IMF archives.

events.title: People's Republic of China admitted to the United Nations
events.description: On 25 October 1971, the UN General Assembly adopted Resolution 2758, recognising the People's Republic of China as the only legitimate representative of China to the United Nations and expelling the representatives of Chiang Kai-shek's Republic of China (Taiwan). The vote was 76 in favour, 35 against, with 17 abstentions. The resolution ended 22 years during which Taiwan had held China's seat, including its permanent seat on the Security Council with veto power. The shift reflected the PRC's growing international recognition, the broader tide of decolonisation (many new African and Asian member states supported Beijing), and the evolving U.S. posture under Nixon's opening to China. The decision permanently altered the UN's power dynamics and left Taiwan in a uniquely ambiguous international legal position.
key_figures: Mao Zedong (Chairman, Chinese Communist Party)
Chiao Kuan-hua (PRC representative to the UN)
George H.W. Bush (U.S. Ambassador to the UN)
Chiang Kai-shek (President, Republic of China on Taiwan)

sources.name: UN General Assembly Resolution 2758 (XXVI), 25 October 1971
UN General Assembly Official Records, 26th Session, 1971
Foot, Rosemary, The Practice of Power: US Relations with China since 1949 (1995)

events.certainty_note: Recorded General Assembly vote with full UN documentation.

events.title: Kissinger's secret visit to Beijing
events.description: On 9–11 July 1971, U.S. National Security Advisor Henry Kissinger secretly flew from Islamabad to Beijing, using a feigned illness as cover during an official visit to Pakistan. In Beijing, he held extensive talks with Premier Zhou Enlai, establishing the framework for Nixon's historic 1972 visit. The mission, codenamed 'Polo,' was facilitated by Pakistani President Yahya Khan, who served as intermediary. Nixon announced the breakthrough to the world on 15 July in a televised address that stunned allies and adversaries alike. The opening fundamentally recalibrated Cold War geopolitics, alarming the Soviet Union and shocking Japan, which had received no advance notice. It also complicated American moral authority regarding the concurrent Pakistani military atrocities in East Pakistan, as Washington relied on Yahya Khan's good offices.
key_figures: Henry Kissinger (U.S. National Security Advisor)
Zhou Enlai (Premier, People's Republic of China)
Richard Nixon (President of the United States)
Yahya Khan (President of Pakistan)

sources.name: Kissinger, Henry, White House Years (1979), ch. 19 'The Opening to China'
National Security Archive, George Washington University: 'The Beijing-Washington Back-Channel' declassified document collection
MacMillan, Margaret, Nixon and Mao: The Week That Changed the World (2007)

events.certainty_note: Declassified memoranda of conversation and extensive memoir accounts from multiple participants.
cross_references: 1971-002 (Bangladesh Liberation War — Yahya Khan as intermediary complicates U.S. response)

events.title: Women's suffrage adopted in Switzerland by referendum
events.description: On 7 February 1971, Swiss male voters approved women's suffrage at the federal level in a referendum, with 65.7% in favour. Switzerland became one of the last European countries to grant women the right to vote and stand for election in national politics, decades after most Western democracies. The change had been rejected by voters in 1959. Women voted for the first time in the October 1971 federal elections, and eleven women were elected to the National Council. However, the canton of Appenzell Innerrhoden continued to deny women the cantonal vote until forced to concede by a Federal Supreme Court ruling in 1990, illustrating the extreme localism of Swiss direct democracy.
key_figures: Emilie Lieberherr (women's rights activist and later Zurich city councillor)
Gertrud Heinzelmann (feminist lawyer and theologian)

sources.name: Schweizerische Bundeskanzlei (Swiss Federal Chancellery), referendum results, 7 February 1971
Banaszak, Lee Ann, Why Movements Succeed or Fail: Opportunity, Culture, and the Struggle for Woman Suffrage (1996)
Studer, Brigitte, et al., Frauen und Staat / Les femmes et l'État (1998)

events.certainty_note: Official referendum results with complete Swiss government documentation.
cross_references: 1990-XXX (Appenzell Innerrhoden forced to grant women's cantonal suffrage)

events.title: Attica Prison uprising
events.description: On 9 September 1971, approximately 1,300 inmates at Attica Correctional Facility in western New York seized control of the prison, taking 42 staff members hostage. The uprising was driven by overcrowding, poor conditions, racial discrimination (the prison population was predominantly Black and Puerto Rican while guards were almost entirely white), and the broader radicalisation of the era. After four days of negotiations, Governor Nelson Rockefeller ordered state police to retake the facility by force on 13 September. The assault killed 29 inmates and 10 hostages — all hostage deaths were caused by law enforcement gunfire, not by inmates, contradicting initial official claims. The massacre became a symbol of systemic racism in the criminal justice system and catalysed prison reform movements.
key_figures: Nelson Rockefeller (Governor of New York)
Russell Oswald (Commissioner, New York State Department of Correctional Services)
L.D. Barkley (inmate spokesman, killed during assault)
Tom Wicker (New York Times journalist, observer during negotiations)

sources.name: McKay Commission Report (New York State Special Commission on Attica, 1972)
Thompson, Heather Ann, Blood in the Water: The Attica Prison Uprising of 1971 and Its Legacy (2016)
Wicker, Tom, A Time to Die: The Attica Prison Revolt (1975)

events.certainty_note: Exhaustively documented event with official commission report, extensive journalism, and later declassified records.

events.title: Intel 4004 microprocessor released
events.description: In November 1971, Intel Corporation commercially released the Intel 4004, the world's first general-purpose microprocessor available on a single chip. Designed by Federico Faggin, Ted Hoff, and Stanley Mazor, the 4-bit processor contained approximately 2,300 transistors on a single silicon die and was originally developed for the Busicom 141-PF calculator. The 4004 could execute approximately 92,000 instructions per second. While its immediate commercial impact was modest, the concept of integrating an entire CPU onto a single chip established the foundational paradigm for the microcomputer revolution. The microprocessor would transform computing from large institutional machines to ubiquitous personal devices over the following decades.
key_figures: Federico Faggin (chip designer, Intel)
Marcian 'Ted' Hoff (architect of the microprocessor concept, Intel)
Stanley Mazor (co-designer, Intel)
Masatoshi Shima (designer at Busicom, later Intel)

sources.name: Intel advertisement in Electronic News, 15 November 1971 ('A New Era of Integrated Electronics')
Faggin, Federico, 'The Making of the First Microprocessor,' IEEE Solid-State Circuits Magazine, Winter 2009
Aspray, William (ed.), Computing Before Computers (1990); updated in IEEE Annals of the History of Computing

events.certainty_note: Commercial product with documented release date, patent filings, and contemporary trade press coverage.

events.title: 26th Amendment ratified: U.S. voting age lowered to 18
events.description: On 1 July 1971, the 26th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution was ratified, lowering the voting age from 21 to 18. It was ratified in the shortest time of any constitutional amendment — just 100 days after congressional approval on 23 March. The amendment was driven by the argument that citizens old enough to be drafted and sent to fight in Vietnam (the draft age was 18) should be able to vote. The Supreme Court's Oregon v. Mitchell decision (1970) had created an inconsistency by allowing Congress to lower the voting age for federal but not state elections, making a constitutional amendment necessary. The amendment enfranchised approximately 11 million new voters.
key_figures: Jennings Randolph (U.S. Senator from West Virginia, longtime advocate)
Richard Nixon (President, certified the amendment)

sources.name: U.S. Constitution, Amendment XXVI (ratified 1 July 1971)
Oregon v. Mitchell, 400 U.S. 112 (1970)
Keyssar, Alexander, The Right to Vote: The Contested History of Democracy in the United States (2000)

events.certainty_note: Constitutional amendment with complete ratification records.

events.title: Lin Biao incident: death of Mao's designated successor
events.description: On 13 September 1971, a Trident jet carrying Marshal Lin Biao — Mao Zedong's constitutionally designated successor — crashed in Öndörkhaan, Mongolia, killing all aboard. The official Chinese account, revealed gradually over subsequent months, claimed that Lin had plotted a coup (codenamed 'Project 571') and, when the plot was discovered, attempted to flee to the Soviet Union. The incident shattered the mythology of Cultural Revolution unity and profoundly damaged the Communist Party's credibility, as Lin had been celebrated as Mao's 'closest comrade-in-arms.' The affair triggered a major political crisis within the CCP and contributed to the gradual erosion of radical Cultural Revolution policies. Many details remain obscure and contested by scholars.
key_figures: Lin Biao (Vice Chairman, CCP; Minister of Defence; designated successor to Mao)
Mao Zedong (Chairman, CCP)
Ye Qun (Lin Biao's wife, killed in crash)
Lin Liguo (Lin Biao's son, alleged coup plotter, killed in crash)
Zhou Enlai (Premier, managed the crisis)

sources.name: CCP Central Committee documents on the Lin Biao incident (Zhongfa [1971] No. 57 and subsequent circulars)
Teiwes, Frederick C. and Sun, Warren, The Tragedy of Lin Biao: Riding the Tiger during the Cultural Revolution, 1966–1971 (1996)
Jin, Qiu, The Culture of Power: The Lin Biao Incident in the Cultural Revolution (1999)

events.certainty_note: The crash itself is confirmed by physical evidence and Mongolian/Soviet investigations. The circumstances and alleged coup plot remain partially contested by scholars, though the CCP's basic narrative is broadly accepted.
cross_references: 1976-XXX (Death of Mao, arrest of Gang of Four)

events.title: Sadat's Corrective Revolution consolidates power in Egypt
events.description: On 15 May 1971, President Anwar Sadat moved decisively against a powerful faction of Nasserist loyalists led by Vice President Ali Sabri, Interior Minister Sha'rawi Gomaa, and War Minister Muhammad Fawzi, who had been plotting to remove him. Sadat pre-empted the conspiracy, arrested the plotters, and purged the Arab Socialist Union of their supporters in what he termed the 'Corrective Revolution' (Thawrat al-Tashih). The move eliminated the last institutional power base of the Nasserist old guard and consolidated Sadat's personal authority. He subsequently promulgated a new permanent constitution in September 1971. This consolidation set the stage for Sadat's dramatic policy shifts: the expulsion of Soviet advisors in 1972, the October War of 1973, and the eventual pivot toward the United States and peace with Israel.
key_figures: Anwar Sadat (President of Egypt)
Ali Sabri (Vice President, arrested)
Sha'rawi Gomaa (Interior Minister, arrested)
Muhammad Fawzi (War Minister, arrested)

sources.name: Sadat, Anwar, In Search of Identity: An Autobiography (1978)
Waterbury, John, The Egypt of Nasser and Sadat: The Political Economy of Two Regimes (1983)
Contemporary reporting in Al-Ahram and international press, May 1971

events.certainty_note: Well-documented political purge with official announcements, trial records, and extensive journalistic coverage.

events.title: United Arab Emirates gains independence
events.description: On 2 December 1971, six of the seven Trucial States — Abu Dhabi, Dubai, Sharjah, Ajman, Umm al-Quwain, and Fujairah — formed the United Arab Emirates as Britain withdrew from its treaty obligations east of Suez. Ras al-Khaimah joined on 10 February 1972. Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan of Abu Dhabi became the first president. The federation was a product of British imperial withdrawal and local rulers' pragmatic calculation that a union offered better security and economic prospects than independence as micro-states. Bahrain and Qatar, originally considered for inclusion, opted for separate independence (August and September 1971, respectively). The new state's vast oil reserves, particularly Abu Dhabi's, would make it one of the wealthiest nations per capita in the world.
key_figures: Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan (Ruler of Abu Dhabi, President of UAE)
Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum (Ruler of Dubai, Vice President of UAE)
Sir Geoffrey Arthur (last British Political Resident in the Persian Gulf)

sources.name: Treaty establishing the United Arab Emirates, 2 December 1971
Heard-Bey, Frauke, From Trucial States to United Arab Emirates: A Society in Transition (1982, revised 2004)
British Foreign and Commonwealth Office records, withdrawal from the Gulf, 1968–1971 (National Archives, Kew)

events.certainty_note: Treaty-based formation with complete diplomatic and legal documentation.
cross_references: 1968-XXX (British announcement of withdrawal east of Suez)

events.title: Qatar and Bahrain gain independence
events.description: Bahrain declared independence on 15 August 1971 and Qatar on 3 September 1971, following the British withdrawal from the Persian Gulf. Both had been British protectorates under treaty arrangements dating to the 19th century. A UN-supervised survey in Bahrain confirmed that the population desired independence rather than union with Iran, which had maintained a historical claim. Qatar, under the leadership of Sheikh Ahmad bin Ali Al Thani, opted for independence rather than joining the proposed federation of Trucial States. Both states signed new treaties of friendship with Britain replacing the old protectorate arrangements. Their independence, along with the formation of the UAE, completed the British withdrawal from the Gulf.
key_figures: Sheikh Isa bin Salman Al Khalifa (Emir of Bahrain)
Sheikh Ahmad bin Ali Al Thani (Emir of Qatar)
Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi (Shah of Iran, relinquished claim on Bahrain)

sources.name: UN General Assembly Resolution 2716 (XXV), 1970 (Bahrain independence survey)
Crystal, Jill, Oil and Politics in the Gulf: Rulers and Merchants in Kuwait and Qatar (1990)
British treaty of friendship with Bahrain and Qatar, 1971 (National Archives, Kew)

events.certainty_note: Treaty-based independence with complete UN and diplomatic documentation.

events.title: Congo renamed Zaire under Mobutu's authenticity campaign
events.description: On 27 October 1971, President Mobutu Sese Seko renamed the Democratic Republic of the Congo as the Republic of Zaire, as part of his broader 'Authenticité' campaign to purge colonial influences and forge a unified national identity. The Congo River was renamed the Zaire River, and Mobutu himself changed his name from Joseph-Désiré Mobutu. The programme mandated the adoption of African names, banned Western-style clothing in favour of the abacost suit, and promoted what Mobutu characterised as authentic African cultural values. While presented as anti-colonial nationalism, the authenticity campaign served primarily to consolidate Mobutu's personal power and create a patronage state. The country would revert to the name Democratic Republic of the Congo after Mobutu's overthrow in 1997.
key_figures: Mobutu Sese Seko (President of Zaire, formerly Joseph-Désiré Mobutu)

sources.name: Presidential ordinance renaming the country, 27 October 1971
Young, Crawford and Turner, Thomas, The Rise and Decline of the Zairian State (1985)
Wrong, Michela, In the Footsteps of Mr. Kurtz: Living on the Brink of Disaster in Mobutu's Congo (2001)

events.certainty_note: Official government action with contemporary documentation and press coverage.

events.title: Soviet Mars 2 and Mars 3 missions reach Mars
events.description: In November–December 1971, the Soviet Union's Mars 2 and Mars 3 spacecraft arrived at Mars. Mars 2's lander crashed on 27 November, becoming the first human-made object to reach the Martian surface, though it returned no data. Mars 3's lander achieved a soft landing on 2 December and transmitted for approximately 20 seconds before falling silent — the first soft landing on Mars, though no useful data was returned, likely due to a massive dust storm then engulfing the planet. Both orbiters successfully entered Mars orbit and returned data on surface temperature, atmospheric composition, and topography over several months. The missions demonstrated Soviet capability in deep-space exploration despite the landing failures.
key_figures: Sergei Afanasyev (Minister of General Machine-Building, oversaw the Soviet space programme)
Georgy Babakin (chief designer of the Lavochkin Association, designer of the probes — died April 1971 before arrival)

sources.name: Soviet Academy of Sciences mission reports, published in Kosmicheskie Issledovaniya (Cosmic Research), 1972
Perminov, V.G., The Difficult Road to Mars: A Brief History of Mars Exploration in the Soviet Union (NASA translation, 1999)

events.certainty_note: Confirmed by Soviet mission records and independent tracking by Western space agencies.

events.title: Mariner 9 becomes first spacecraft to orbit another planet
events.description: On 14 November 1971, NASA's Mariner 9 entered orbit around Mars, becoming the first spacecraft to successfully orbit another planet. It arrived during a global dust storm that obscured the surface, but as the storm cleared, the probe returned 7,329 images over its operational life, mapping 85% of the Martian surface at resolutions far exceeding any previous mission. Mariner 9 revealed Olympus Mons (the solar system's largest known volcano), the vast Valles Marineris canyon system (named after the spacecraft), evidence of ancient river channels suggesting past water flow, and detailed views of the Martian moons Phobos and Deimos. The mission fundamentally transformed scientific understanding of Mars from a static, Moon-like body to a geologically complex world.
key_figures: Dan Schneiderman (Mariner 9 project manager, JPL)

sources.name: NASA Jet Propulsion Laboratory, Mariner Mars 1971 Project Final Report (JPL Technical Report 32-1550, 1973)
Hartmann, William K. and Raper, Odell, The New Mars: The Discoveries of Mariner 9 (NASA SP-337, 1974)

events.certainty_note: NASA mission with complete telemetry and publicly released imagery.

events.title: Apollo 14 and Apollo 15 lunar missions
events.description: NASA conducted two crewed lunar missions in 1971. Apollo 14 (31 January – 9 February) landed at Fra Mauro, the target originally intended for the aborted Apollo 13, with Alan Shepard and Edgar Mitchell walking on the lunar surface while Stuart Roosa orbited above. Apollo 15 (26 July – 7 August) represented a major leap in capability as the first 'J-mission,' featuring the Lunar Roving Vehicle, extended surface stays, and enhanced scientific instrumentation. Commander David Scott and Lunar Module Pilot James Irwin explored the Hadley-Apennine region, collecting 77 kg of samples including the famous 'Genesis Rock,' an anorthosite specimen approximately 4 billion years old. Apollo 15 also deployed the first subsatellite in lunar orbit.
key_figures: Alan Shepard (Commander, Apollo 14)
Edgar Mitchell (Lunar Module Pilot, Apollo 14)
David Scott (Commander, Apollo 15)
James Irwin (Lunar Module Pilot, Apollo 15)
Alfred Worden (Command Module Pilot, Apollo 15)

sources.name: NASA Apollo 14 and Apollo 15 Mission Reports (MSC-04112, 1971; MSC-05161, 1971)
Scott, David and Leonov, Alexei, Two Sides of the Moon (2004)

events.certainty_note: NASA missions with complete mission records, telemetry, photography, and returned samples.

events.title: Greenpeace founded in Vancouver
events.description: In 1971, a group of environmental and anti-nuclear activists in Vancouver, British Columbia, organised as the 'Don't Make a Wave Committee' and sailed a chartered fishing vessel, the Phyllis Cormack, toward the U.S. nuclear test site at Amchitka Island in the Aleutians to protest a planned underground nuclear detonation. Though the vessel was intercepted by the U.S. Coast Guard before reaching the test zone, the voyage generated enormous media attention and public sympathy. The organisation formally adopted the name Greenpeace later that year. The Amchitka test (Cannikin) proceeded on 6 November but was the last nuclear test conducted on the island, and the site was subsequently decommissioned. Greenpeace would grow into one of the world's most prominent environmental organisations.
key_figures: Irving Stowe (co-founder, Don't Make a Wave Committee)
Jim Bohlen (co-founder)
Ben Metcalfe (journalist and activist aboard the Phyllis Cormack)
Bob Hunter (journalist, later Greenpeace president)

sources.name: Contemporary reporting in Vancouver Sun and Canadian press, September–November 1971
Hunter, Robert, The Greenpeace to Amchitka: An Environmental Odyssey (2004, posthumous edition)
Zelko, Frank, Make It a Green Peace!: The Rise of Countercultural Environmentalism (2013)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented founding with contemporary press coverage, though exact organisational dates are somewhat fluid as the name evolved gradually.

events.title: Aswan High Dam completed in Egypt
events.description: On 15 January 1971, the Aswan High Dam was officially inaugurated by President Sadat and Soviet President Podgorny, completing a project begun in 1960 with massive Soviet financial and technical assistance. The dam, 111 metres high and 3,830 metres across, created Lake Nasser, one of the world's largest artificial lakes. It provided Egypt with flood control, a reliable irrigation water supply, and approximately 2.1 gigawatts of hydroelectric generating capacity — roughly half of Egypt's electricity at the time. However, the dam also ended the Nile's annual flood cycle that had deposited fertile silt for millennia, leading to downstream soil degradation, increased salinity in the delta, and coastal erosion. The UNESCO-led international campaign to relocate the Abu Simbel and Philae temples, threatened by the rising reservoir, had been completed in 1968.
key_figures: Anwar Sadat (President of Egypt)
Nikolai Podgorny (President of the Presidium of the Supreme Soviet)
Gamal Abdel Nasser (former President, initiated the project — deceased 1970)

sources.name: Inauguration ceremony records and contemporary Egyptian and Soviet press, January 1971
Waterbury, John, Hydropolitics of the Nile Valley (1979)
Abu-Zeid, Mahmoud and El-Shibini, F.Z., 'Egypt's High Aswan Dam,' International Journal of Water Resources Development, 13:2 (1997)

events.certainty_note: Major engineering project with complete construction and inauguration records.
cross_references: 1956-XXX (Suez Crisis partly triggered by Aswan Dam financing dispute)

events.title: South Asian refugee crisis: 10 million flee to India
events.description: Between March and December 1971, approximately 10 million refugees — predominantly Bengali Hindus but also Bengali Muslims — fled the Pakistani military's campaign in East Pakistan into the Indian states of West Bengal, Tripura, Meghalaya, and Assam. This constituted one of the largest and most rapid refugee movements in recorded history. The influx placed enormous strain on India's resources and infrastructure, with refugee camps stretching along the border. The crisis was a significant factor in India's decision to intervene militarily in December 1971. International relief efforts were complicated by Cold War politics, with the United States and China supporting Pakistan while the Soviet Union backed India. The humanitarian catastrophe drew worldwide attention through reporting by journalists and the Concert for Bangladesh organised by George Harrison.
key_figures: Indira Gandhi (Prime Minister of India)
George Harrison (musician, organised Concert for Bangladesh benefit)
Ravi Shankar (musician, co-organised Concert for Bangladesh)
Sen. Edward Kennedy (U.S. Senator, visited refugee camps)

sources.name: UNHCR and International Red Cross reports on the East Pakistan refugee crisis, 1971
Bass, Gary J., The Blood Telegram: Nixon, Kissinger, and a Forgotten Genocide (2013)
Government of India, Bangladesh Documents (Ministry of External Affairs, 1971)

events.certainty_note: Documented by UN agencies, international relief organisations, and extensive journalism. Exact numbers are estimates but the order of magnitude is not disputed.

events.title: India–Soviet Treaty of Peace, Friendship and Cooperation
events.description: On 9 August 1971, India and the Soviet Union signed a twenty-year Treaty of Peace, Friendship and Cooperation in New Delhi. While formally not a military alliance, Article IX committed each party to refrain from assisting an aggressor against the other and to engage in mutual consultations in the event of a threat. The treaty was a direct response to the U.S.–China–Pakistan alignment that was crystallising through Kissinger's diplomatic manoeuvres. It provided India with a security guarantee against potential Chinese intervention during the looming conflict with Pakistan over East Bengal. The treaty represented a significant tilt in Indian non-alignment toward the Soviet bloc and shaped South Asian geopolitics for the remainder of the Cold War.
key_figures: Indira Gandhi (Prime Minister of India)
Andrei Gromyko (Soviet Foreign Minister)
Swaran Singh (Indian External Affairs Minister)

sources.name: Treaty of Peace, Friendship and Cooperation between India and the USSR, 9 August 1971 (text in Indian Treaty Series)
Raghavan, Srinath, 1971: A Global History of the Creation of Bangladesh (2013)

events.certainty_note: Published treaty text with full diplomatic documentation.

events.title: Medellín document and liberation theology advance in Latin America
events.description: Throughout 1971, the liberation theology movement gained institutional momentum in Latin America. Gustavo Gutiérrez published his foundational work 'Teología de la liberación: Perspectivas' in Lima, systematising the theological framework that had emerged from the 1968 Medellín Conference of Latin American bishops. The book argued that Christian salvation required active engagement with the structural causes of poverty and injustice. Liberation theology became deeply influential across Latin America, inspiring base ecclesial communities, political activism, and fierce controversy within the Catholic Church. Conservative critics accused the movement of Marxist infiltration, while proponents argued it represented an authentic reading of the Gospel's 'preferential option for the poor.'
key_figures: Gustavo Gutiérrez (Peruvian Dominican priest and theologian)
Hélder Câmara (Archbishop of Olinda and Recife, Brazil)

sources.name: Gutiérrez, Gustavo, Teología de la liberación: Perspectivas (1971)
Smith, Christian, The Emergence of Liberation Theology: Radical Religion and Social Movement Theory (1991)

events.certainty_note: Published text with established publication date. The broader movement's dating is well documented through Church records and publications.

events.title: Sudanese communist coup attempt and purge
events.description: On 19 July 1971, communist-aligned military officers briefly overthrew President Jaafar Nimeiry in a coup. Nimeiry was restored to power within three days with support from Egypt and Libya, which intercepted a BOAC flight carrying coup leaders attempting to flee. The failed coup was followed by a ferocious purge of the Sudanese Communist Party, one of the largest and best-organised communist parties in the Arab world and Africa. Party leader Abdel Khaliq Mahjub and other senior figures were executed. The crackdown effectively destroyed the SCP as a political force and marked Nimeiry's turn to the right, away from the leftist coalition that had initially brought him to power in 1969. The event contributed to the broader pattern of anti-communist suppression across the Arab world.
key_figures: Jaafar Nimeiry (President of Sudan)
Abdel Khaliq Mahjub (General Secretary, Sudanese Communist Party, executed)
Hashim al-Atta (coup leader, executed)
Muammar Gaddafi (Libya, assisted Nimeiry's restoration)

sources.name: Contemporary reporting in The Times (London), Le Monde, and New York Times, July 1971
Niblock, Tim, Class and Power in Sudan: The Dynamics of Sudanese Politics, 1898–1985 (1987)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented coup and counter-coup with extensive contemporary press coverage and diplomatic records.

events.title: UK decimalisation of currency
events.description: On 15 February 1971, known as 'Decimal Day,' the United Kingdom and Ireland converted their currencies to a decimal system, replacing the centuries-old system of pounds, shillings, and pence (£sd, with 12 pence to a shilling and 20 shillings to a pound, yielding 240 pence per pound) with a new system of 100 new pence to the pound. The changeover had been planned since the Decimal Currency Act 1969 and was preceded by years of public information campaigns and parallel pricing. Despite widespread public anxiety, the transition was accomplished with relatively little disruption, aided by a six-month dual-pricing period. The old system, which had its roots in Carolingian monetary conventions, was the last major pre-decimal currency system in Western Europe.
key_figures: Lord Fiske (Chairman, Decimal Currency Board)

sources.name: Decimal Currency Act 1969 (UK Parliament)
Decimal Currency Board, Final Report (1971)
Bank of England historical records on decimalisation

events.certainty_note: Official government action with complete legislative and administrative documentation.

disconfirming_evidence: Several events listed above have contested elements. The death toll in the Bangladesh Liberation War (1971-002) is deeply disputed: Bangladesh's official figure of 3 million deaths is considered an overestimate by most independent scholars, with figures ranging from 300,000 to 3 million depending on methodology and political perspective — Sarmila Bose's 'Dead Reckoning' (2011) controversially argued for lower figures and was criticised for methodology but highlighted the politicisation of casualty statistics. The Lin Biao incident (1971-011) remains partially opaque: some scholars, notably Jin Qiu, question whether Lin actually planned a coup or was set up by Mao, and the 'Project 571 Outline' document may have been fabricated or exaggerated after the fact. The narrative of Greenpeace's founding (1971-019) is contested among the founders themselves regarding credit and the organisation's exact genesis — the name evolved gradually and the 'Don't Make a Wave Committee' was not formally renamed Greenpeace until slightly later, with different accounts from different participants. The Intel 4004's status as the 'first microprocessor' (1971-009) has been challenged by supporters of the Texas Instruments TMS 1000, which was developed concurrently, and by the earlier Four-Phase Systems AL1 designed by Lee Boysel in 1969, though the 4004 is generally recognised as the first commercially available general-purpose microprocessor on a single chip. The Corrective Revolution in Egypt (1971-012) is described almost entirely through Sadat's own narrative; the degree to which the 'conspiracy' was genuine versus manufactured as a pretext for purging rivals remains debated among scholars.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1971 is extremely rich, with abundant primary documentation including government archives, UN records, diplomatic cables (many now declassified), contemporary journalism from multiple countries, and memoir literature. However, significant biases shape the accessible record. Cold War framing dominates English-language sources, with events interpreted through superpower competition. The Bangladesh conflict's historiography is intensely politicised, with Bangladeshi, Pakistani, and Indian national narratives offering sharply divergent accounts. Chinese sources on the Lin Biao affair remain heavily censored and politically controlled. Soviet space programme records, while substantially opened since 1991, still contain gaps. African and Middle Eastern events tend to receive less granular coverage in English-language scholarship compared to events involving Western powers. The dominance of American sources creates a gravitational pull toward U.S.-centric framing even for events with global significance — the Nixon Shock and Pentagon Papers receive outsized attention relative to, for example, the Gulf states' independence or internal African political dynamics.
